name: Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS)
treatment: Rest at current altitude for 1-2 days
Take Ibuprofen or Paracetamol for headache
If symptoms worsen: DESCEND at least 500-1000m
warning: If symptoms persist or worsen after 24 hours of rest, descend immediately. AMS can progress to life-threatening HACE or HAPE.

name: High Altitude Cerebral Edema (HACE)
treatment: Administer Dexamethasone 8mg initially, then 4mg every 6 hours
Call for helicopter evacuation if descent is not possible
warning: HACE is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Death can occur within 24 hours without treatment. Descend immediately, even at night.

name: High Altitude Pulmonary Edema (HAPE)
symptoms: Persistent dry cough, may become wet
warning: HAPE kills more trekkers than any other altitude illness. Do NOT delay descent. Condition worsens rapidly.

name: Blisters
symptoms: Hot spot or rubbing sensation (early warning)
treatment: Do NOT pop small blisters - they heal faster intact
For large/painful blisters: sterilize needle, drain fluid from edge, keep skin intact as a natural bandage
Cover with moleskin donut or blister-specific bandage (Compeed)
Change dressing daily and keep clean
prevention: 🔑 TAPING: Apply Leukotape P or KT Tape to hotspots BEFORE blisters form
⚠️ Duct tape works in emergencies but can irritate skin - use as last resort
Wear well-fitted, broken-in boots (never trek in new boots)
Use moisture-wicking liner socks under wool hiking socks
Apply foot powder or anti-chafe balm (Body Glide) to problem areas
Keep feet dry - change socks at lunch if sweaty
Lace boots snugly downhill to prevent toe blisters

name: Trekker's Knee
symptoms: Pain around or behind the kneecap
treatment: Rest: Stop and elevate the leg for 15-20 minutes
Ice: Apply cold pack or cold stream water for 15 min
Compression: Use elastic bandage or knee brace
Use trekking poles to offload 20-30% of knee stress
If severe, consider turning back or arranging transport
prevention: 🔑 POLES: Use trekking poles on ALL descents - reduces knee stress by 25%
🔑 TECHNIQUE: Take small, controlled steps downhill - never rush
Strengthen quads and hamstrings before your trek
Warm up with gentle stretches each morning
Reduce pack weight as much as possible
Wear supportive boots with good cushioning
Consider knee braces or compression sleeves if prone to issues
warning: Continuing to trek on a painful knee can cause long-term damage. Listen to your body and descend if pain is severe.

name: Ankle Sprain
treatment: Follow RICE: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation
Apply cold compress for 15-20 min every 2-3 hours
Wrap with elastic bandage (not too tight)
prevention: Use trekking poles on uneven terrain
Watch your footing on loose rocks

name: Suspected Fracture
treatment: Do NOT attempt to realign the bone
Immobilize the limb using splint (trekking poles, sticks)
Call for evacuation - do not walk on leg fractures
prevention: Take care on slippery or loose surfaces
warning: If bone is protruding (open fracture), cover with sterile dressing, do NOT push back in. Seek immediate evacuation.

name: Hypothermia
treatment: Warm the core first (chest, neck, head, groin)
Use body-to-body contact in sleeping bag
Give warm sweet drinks if conscious
Do NOT rub limbs or give alcohol
prevention: Stay dry - change wet clothes immediately
warning: Severe hypothermia can cause heart arrhythmia. Handle patient very gently. Evacuate immediately.

name: Frostbite
symptoms: Numbness in fingers, toes, ears, nose
Skin appears white, waxy, or grayish
treatment: Get to shelter - do NOT thaw if refreezing is possible
Rewarm in warm water (37-39°C) for 30-60 min
Do NOT rub or massage affected area
Give pain medication - thawing is painful
prevention: Avoid tight boots that restrict circulation
warning: Never walk on frostbitten feet. Once thawed, tissue is extremely fragile and must not freeze again.

name: Severe Sunburn / Snow Blindness
symptoms: Snow blindness: gritty feeling, tearing, light sensitivity
treatment: Apply cool compresses to burned areas
Apply aloe vera or moisturizing lotion
For snow blindness: rest in dark room, cold compresses on eyes
prevention: Wear SPF 50+ sunscreen, reapply every 2 hours

name: Dehydration
treatment: Add oral rehydration salts if available
prevention: Drink 3-4 liters per day at altitude
Monitor urine color (should be light yellow)
Start hydrating before you feel thirsty

name: Traveler's Diarrhea
treatment: Stay hydrated with oral rehydration salts
Take Loperamide for symptom relief if needed
prevention: Drink only purified or bottled water
Avoid raw vegetables and unpeeled fruits

name: Cuts and Wounds
treatment: Apply direct pressure to stop bleeding
Clean with clean water or antiseptic
Change dressing daily and monitor for infection
prevention: Wear gloves when handling sharp equipment
Be careful with knives and tools
warning: Signs of infection: increasing redness, swelling, warmth, pus, red streaks, fever. Seek medical help if present.
